planting.note: Divide clumps in early spring or fall in moist, humus-rich, well-drained soil in partial to deep shade; loose-flowered woodland sedge grows naturally in rich mesic forests and forested slopes throughout eastern North America. It is one of the most shade-tolerant native sedges.

directions: Plant in moist to well-drained, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial to deep shade; loose-flowered woodland sedge is one of the finest native groundcover sedges for deep shade conditions in eastern North American woodland gardens, producing graceful, arching, medium-width leaves of fresh green that provide attractive texture throughout the growing season. The nodding flower spikes in spring are loosely arranged — giving the species its name — and have a delicate, casual elegance. It grows naturally in rich mesic forests, forested slopes, and streamside woodlands on fertile, well-drained to moist soils. Substantially more shade-tolerant than lawn alternatives and an excellent low-maintenance substitute for turf beneath mature trees where grass struggles. Maintains attractive appearance with minimal water or care once established. Pair with native ferns, wild ginger (Asarum), and trilliums for a natural-looking native groundcover.

planting.note: Divide clumps in spring in dry to moist, calcium-rich, humus-rich, well-drained soil in partial to deep shade; broadleaf woodland sedge is adapted to rocky, calcareous (limestone-derived) forest floors and also grows in more neutral upland forests throughout its range.

directions: Plant in dry to moderately moist, humus-rich, neutral to slightly alkaline, well-drained soil in partial to deep shade; broadleaf woodland sedge is one of the most distinctive native shade sedges of the eastern United States, notable for its unusually wide, almost ribbon-like leaf blades that are significantly broader than other shade sedges — an immediately recognizable trait at close range. The foliage is an attractive blue-green to glaucous color, adding a cool, pale note to the shade garden. It grows naturally on rocky, calcium-rich forest floors associated with limestone or calcareous soils in rich mesic woodlands. Extremely shade tolerant and drought resistant once established — a rare combination that makes it an outstanding low-maintenance groundcover under dense mature tree canopies where both shade and summer drought are challenging. Excellent with other dry-shade natives like wild ginger and Solomon's seal.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in moist to wet, sandy, acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade; white-topped sedge grows naturally in the wet pine flatwoods, bogs, and seepage slopes of the Gulf and Atlantic Coastal Plain. Excellent in rain gardens and wet-site landscapes in zone 9.

directions: Plant in moist to wet, sandy or peaty, acidic soil in full sun to partial shade; white-topped sedge is one of the most ornamentally distinctive native sedges of the Gulf and Atlantic Coastal Plain, producing a starburst of narrow white bracts (leaf-like structures) surrounding the inconspicuous true flowers — creating a bold, star-shaped white ornamental display that is visually striking from a distance. The white bracts are produced prolifically on rush-like, fine-textured dark green foliage from late spring through summer. It grows naturally in wet, sandy, acidic flatwoods and boggy seepage slopes of the coastal plain from Maryland to Texas. In rain gardens, pond margins, and boggy areas in zone 9, it is a superb native ornamental that blooms when few other wetland plants have such visual impact. The plant spreads by rhizomes to form attractive colonies in appropriate wet conditions. A non-showy but excellent addition to native-themed wet gardens.

planting.note: Scarify seeds and sow indoors 8 weeks before last frost; transplant in spring in well-drained, fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun. Golden shower trees begin flowering at 3–5 years from seed. In zone 9, they may defoliate after hard freezes but re-sprout from the roots.

directions: Plant in well-drained, fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun in frost-free to near-frost-free zones 10–12; golden shower tree is one of the most spectacular of all flowering trees — producing extraordinary, long, pendant clusters of brilliant golden-yellow flowers in spring before or during leaf emergence, creating the impression of a tree draped in cascading gold. The national tree of Thailand, it is celebrated throughout tropical Asia and the Caribbean, where brief dry seasons cue simultaneous, spectacular flowering on leafless trees. The long, cylindrical, dark brown seed pods (up to 24 inches long) contain sweet, sticky pulp surrounding the seeds, historically used medicinally as a laxative throughout tropical medicine traditions. In zone 9 it is marginally hardy — surviving mild winters and re-sprouting after damage but rarely flowering reliably. Best treated as a large container specimen in zone 9 or grown in the warmest microclimates of zone 10.

planting.note: Plant container-grown grafted plants in spring in well-drained, lean to average, neutral to alkaline soil in full sun; Indian jujube is more heat- and drought-tolerant but less cold-hardy than Chinese jujube (Ziziphus jujuba). Grafted plants bear fruit much earlier than seedlings.

directions: Plant in well-drained, lean to average, neutral to alkaline soil in full sun in zones 9–12; Indian jujube is one of the most widely cultivated fruits of the tropical and subtropical world, producing abundant crops of round to oval fruits that ripen from green to yellow, red, or purplish-black depending on cultivar — eaten fresh when crisp and apple-like, or dried when they become wrinkled and date-like in flavor. It is more heat-adapted and tolerant of alkaline soils than Chinese jujube, making it suited to south Texas, the Rio Grande Valley, and Gulf Coast zone 9–10 gardens. The fruit is one of the most important traditional fruits of South Asia, the Middle East, and West Africa, and is now gaining attention in the US as a dual-season crop that produces prolifically even under drought stress. Named cultivars such as 'Umran', 'Gola', and 'Seb' are selected for large fruit size and superior flavor.
